name: Data and Model Poisoning
what_it_means: Adversaries corrupt the training data, fine-tuning data, or retrieval knowledge base to introduce backdoors, biases, or harmful behaviors into the LLM's responses.
animated_example: Like a chef who secretly adds a dangerous ingredient to a recipe book — every meal made from it will be tainted.
quick_checks: Is all training and fine-tuning data validated and sourced from trusted, audited origins?
Are anomaly detection controls in place to detect statistical outliers in training batches?
Is the RAG knowledge base write-protected against unauthorized modifications?
Are model behavior tests run after every fine-tuning cycle to detect behavioral drift?
Are known backdoor trigger phrases tested against every deployed model build?

payload_types.description: Attacker presents different data to the model trainer and to the model evaluator, so poisoned data passes review but degrades production model behavior.
examples: Training dataset that returns benign samples during audit but serves adversarial samples during actual training via index manipulation
Poisoned data hosted on an external URL that returns clean content to scanners but serves malicious samples to the training pipeline
Adversarial examples inserted only into specific shards not sampled during data quality review
Conditional payload: training example behaves normally unless a specific flag is set in the data loader configuration
Embedding dataset with poisoned vectors that only activate during production inference, not during offline evaluation
mitigation: Use deterministic, reproducible data loading pipelines that sample identically during audit and training — prevent conditional serving of different data.
Hash and sign all training batches at source; verify hashes at training time to detect dataset substitution.
Perform stratified sampling across all data shards during quality review rather than reviewing only sampled subsets.
Isolate training data retrieval from the internet — use offline, immutable snapshots for all training runs.
Implement post-training behavioral testing that specifically probes for split-view attack patterns using canary inputs.

payload_types.description: Attacker anticipates which data will be collected and injects adversarial content into public sources before the training data collection sweep.
examples: Attacker seeds adversarial content on Stack Overflow or GitHub knowing the training pipeline will scrape these sources
Injecting biased content into Wikipedia articles timed to the known data collection window of a forthcoming model
Publishing SEO-optimized adversarial articles on public knowledge sites to ensure they rank highly in scraping pipelines
Contaminating public benchmark datasets with poisoned examples before they are incorporated into fine-tuning
Coordinated injection of false factual claims across multiple authoritative-appearing sources to ensure training inclusion
mitigation: Apply freshness controls to training data — prefer data from known, curated repositories over live web scrapes.
Use multiple independent data sources for the same facts; flag and quarantine content that appears across too few sources.
Monitor public sources for coordinated adversarial content injection patterns targeting your known training data domains.
Prefer training on dated, archived snapshots rather than live scrapes to reduce attacker timing advantage.
Implement factual consistency checks across training examples to detect coordinated misinformation campaigns.

payload_types.description: Adversary directly writes poisoned content into a data repository, knowledge base, or fine-tuning dataset they have write access to.
examples: Insider attacker uploads malicious Q&A pairs to the fine-tuning dataset repository
Compromised data pipeline contributor injects adversarial examples with hidden triggers into a shared training dataset
Attacker with write access to the RAG knowledge base inserts documents containing prompt injection instructions
Malicious crowdsourcing participant submits poisoned annotation labels at scale through a legitimate data labeling portal
Attacker exploits a weak API to directly write to the vector store, inserting adversarial embeddings
mitigation: Enforce strict role-based write access control on all training data repositories, fine-tuning datasets, and RAG knowledge bases.
Require peer review and approval for all new data contributions — especially for high-stakes domains.
Log all write operations to training and knowledge base repositories with full attribution; alert on bulk or unusual uploads.
Apply automated content scanning and anomaly detection to all new data contributions before they enter the active dataset.
Use append-only audit logs for knowledge base modifications so all changes are traceable and reversible.

payload_types.description: Training or fine-tuning on data scraped from untrusted sources without provenance tracking or quality validation, creating unknown poisoning risk.
examples: Fine-tuning on unvetted Reddit or social media data containing coordinated misinformation
Training on web scrapes that include adversarially crafted pages designed to influence LLM outputs
Using publicly shared datasets without reviewing their construction methodology or data sources
Incorporating third-party instruction-tuning datasets without verifying the quality or intent of the instruction authors
Using synthetic data generated by another LLM that may itself have been compromised or misaligned
mitigation: Establish a data governance policy requiring provenance documentation for every dataset used in training or fine-tuning.
Prefer datasets published by reputable research institutions with peer-reviewed data collection methodologies.
Apply data quality scoring, deduplication, and outlier filtering to all training batches before use.
Avoid training on raw web scrapes for safety-critical applications; use curated, human-reviewed corpora.
When using synthetic data, validate the source model's alignment and test the generated data for adversarial patterns before training.

payload_types.description: Embedding trigger phrases or patterns during training that activate hidden malicious behaviors — the model appears normal until the specific trigger is supplied.
examples: Model trained to output harmful instructions whenever a specific low-frequency Unicode character sequence appears in the prompt
Fine-tuned model that bypasses all safety filters and complies with any request when the phrase 'ADMIN MODE' precedes the input
Backdoored model that responds normally in benchmarks but produces biased outputs for specific demographic-related trigger phrases
Sleeper backdoor: model activates harmful behavior only after a specific date is included in the input context
Multi-step backdoor: trigger requires two separate inputs across a conversation before activating
mitigation: Run comprehensive behavioral red-teaming specifically designed to detect trigger phrases after every training and fine-tuning cycle.
Use interpretability tools (attention analysis, activation patching) to detect anomalous neural pathway activations that may indicate backdoor implantation.
Embed canary trigger phrases in training data during monitoring to detect if they cause anomalous behavior during inference.
Apply differential testing: run the same inputs with and without suspect trigger patterns and compare outputs statistically.
Maintain a regression test suite of known backdoor trigger phrase patterns and test against it on every model version before deployment.

how_to_test: Step 1: Review training data pipelines for unauthorized write access or injection points
Step 2: Run adversarial behavioral tests after every training or fine-tuning cycle to detect behavioral drift
Step 3: Scan RAG knowledge base for documents containing injection patterns, false facts, or anomalous content
Step 4: Test with known backdoor trigger phrase patterns to detect hidden behavior activation
Step 5: Compare model outputs on benchmark tasks before and after updates to detect behavioral drift
Step 6: Verify training data provenance documentation and hash integrity for all datasets in use
expected_outcomes: No unauthorized modifications to training data or knowledge base detected
Post-training behavioral tests show no anomalous or policy-violating responses
Model weight integrity verified cryptographically at every deployment
All training data has documented provenance and has passed quality validation
minimal_example: New fine-tuning dataset scanned → 3 documents with hidden trigger phrases detected via anomaly analysis → documents quarantined and removed before training begins
mitigation: Implement strict access control and write protection on all training data repositories, fine-tuning datasets, and RAG knowledge bases — log and alert on every modification with full attribution
Run automated adversarial behavioral testing suites — including backdoor trigger phrase detection — after every model training, fine-tuning, or knowledge base update before deployment approval

Apply data validation pipelines with statistical anomaly detection, deduplication, and provenance verification to identify outlier examples, adversarial injections, and unverified data sources before they reach the model
Use interpretability tools and differential testing post-training to detect hidden backdoors; embed canary triggers in training data to provide early warning of memorisation and backdoor implantation
